1 Setup

1.1 Environment and global parameters

Code 
rm(list = ls())   # Clean environment
gc()              # Release memory
          used (Mb) gc trigger (Mb) max used (Mb)
Ncells  623209 33.3    1338545 71.5   797964 42.7
Vcells 1186798  9.1    8388608 64.0  1980051 15.2
Code 
# --- Global parameters --------------------------------------------------------
image_number <- 1
#output_path  <- "output/GSE262881/SCT"
output_path <- "output/GSE262881/LogNorm"
# Data paths
DATA_DIR  <- "../rawdata/GSE262881_RAW"

# Random seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)
options(scipen = 3, digits = 3)

# Increase memory limit for future package
options(future.globals.maxSize = 14000 * 1024^2) # 16 GB limit
# Memory: sequential plan (i7-7560U: 2 physical cores, 16 GB RAM)
# Parallel overhead outweighs benefits on 2-core hardware; protects RAM
library(future)
#plan("multisession", workers = 4) # Use 4 workers
plan("sequential") # Sequential to save RAM

# --- Logging ------------------------------------------------------------------
logger   <- setup_logging("Logger")

log_msg("Starting Merging & Normalization — RAM: ", ram_mb(), " MB")
[19:06:08] Starting Merging & Normalization — RAM: 1065 MB

2 Input data

Dataset: GSE262881 — Wang et al., BMC Biology (2025) 23:124
Paper: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas of all cell types in the brain of 5xFAD Alzheimer mice in response to dietary inulin supplementation

2.1 Full Processing Workflow (per Methods, p. 19)

FASTQ  →  CellRanger 7.1.0  →  raw_feature_bc_matrix/
                                        ↓
                              CellBender 0.2.2
                                        ↓
                    *_CellBender_feature_bc_matrix_filtered.h5   ← .h5 files on GEO

FASTQ  →  CellRanger 7.1.0 (BAM)  →  velocyto 0.17.17
                                        ↓
                                    *.loom.gz   ← .loom files on GEO

We start by reading in the data. The Read10X() function reads in the output of the cellranger pipeline from 10X, returning a unique molecular identified (UMI) count matrix. The values in this matrix represent the number of molecules for each feature (i.e. gene; row) that are detected in each cell (column). Note that more recent versions of cellranger now also output using the h5 file format, which can be read in using the Read10X_h5() function in Seurat.

Data from Wang et al., BMC Biology (2025) 23:124 comes from CellBender filtering. The paper check 2 conditions, Control vs. Insulin; in 4 different parts of brain: we will center just in one region, Forebrain, as example. More regions (translated in more libraries demand more computational power).

2.2 Read 10X matrices (with checkpoint)

Each library is read and saved individuallyto avoid holding all count matrices in RAM simultaneously.

Code 
# define output_path y CHECKPOINT_PREFIX in global_variables                                                                                                    

# Checkpoint: all raw Seurat objects
# With checkpoint (recomended — 24 h5 file would take longer)    
  #seurat_list <- load_h5_samples(data_path, checkpoint = "01_raw_data")      

# Only desired region, with checkpoint
 # seurat_list <- load_h5_samples(DATA_DIR, regions = "forebrain", checkpoint = "01_raw_data")
# Use BPCells library for high efficiency, with checkpoint
seurat_list <- load_h5_samples_bpcells(data_path = DATA_DIR, regions = "forebrain", checkpoint = "01_raw_data_bp", specie = "mouse")

2.3 Import summary

Code 
# Quick check of matching quality (cells found in metadata)
import_tbl <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(seurat_list), function(nm) {
  obj <- seurat_list[[nm]]
  n_total     <- ncol(obj)
  data.frame(Library = nm, N_Cells = n_total,
             row.names = NULL)
}))

3 QC Metrics Calculation

Add human-specific QC metrics. Key difference from mouse data: mitochondrial genes use ^MT- (uppercase).

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("02_seurat_qc")) {
  seurat_qc <- load_checkpoint("02_seurat_qc")
} else {
    if (!exists("seurat_list"))
      seurat_list <- load_checkpoint("01_raw_data_bp")
    seurat_qc <- lapply(seurat_list, function(x) {
      add_qc_metrics(x, pattern_mito = mito_pattern, pattern_ribo = ribo_pattern)
    })
    rm(seurat_list)
    gc()
    save_checkpoint(seurat_qc, "02_seurat_qc")
  }
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_02_seurat_qc.rds  (19:06:11)
Code 
rm(seurat_list)

3.1.1 Distribution of features

3.1.1.1 nCount_RNA distribution per library (pre-filter)

Code 
ncount_rna_prefilter <- ggplot(combined_meta_prefilter) +
  aes(x = "", y = nCount_RNA, fill = library) +
  geom_violin(adjust = 1L) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.01, size=0.02) +
  scale_fill_hue(direction = 1) +
  ggthemes::theme_few() +
  facet_wrap(vars(library))+
  #remove legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(x = "", y = "nCount_RNA")

print(ncount_rna_prefilter)
Code

rm(ncount_rna_prefilter)

3.1.1.7 UMI counts distribution

The UMI counts per cell (nCount_RNA) should generally be above 500, that is the low end of what we expect. If UMI counts are between 500-1000 counts, it is usable but the cells probably should have been sequenced more deeply.

Code 
p_UMI_dist <- combined_meta_prefilter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nCount_RNA, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "UMI distribution per library (pre-filter)",
       x = "nCount_RNA (log10)", y = "Density")

print(p_UMI_dist)
Code 
plots_prefilter[["p_UMI_dist"]] <- p_UMI_dist

rm(p_UMI_dist)

3.1.1.8 Genes detected per cell

We have similar expectations for gene detection as for UMI detection, although it may be a bit lower than UMIs. For high quality data, the proportional histogram should contain a single large peak that represents cells that were encapsulated. If we see a small shoulder to the right of the major peak (not present in our data), or a bimodal distribution of the cells, that can indicate a couple of things. It might be that there are a set of cells that failed for some reason. It could also be that there are biologically different types of cells (i.e. quiescent cell populations, less complex cells of interest), and/or one type is much smaller than the other (i.e. cells with high counts may be cells that are larger in size). Therefore, this threshold should be assessed with other metrics that we describe in this lesson.

Code 
p_genes_dist <-  combined_meta_prefilter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nFeature_RNA, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of number of genes (pre-filter)",
       x = "nFeature_RNA", y = "Density")


print(p_genes_dist)
Code 
plots_prefilter[["p_genes_dist"]] <- p_genes_dist

rm(p_genes_dist)

3.1.1.9 Mitochondrial percentage

This metric can identify whether there is a large amount of mitochondrial contamination from dead or dying cells. We define poor quality samples for mitochondrial counts as cells which surpass the 0.2 mitochondrial ratio mark, unless of course you are expecting this in your sample.

Code 
p_mt_dist <- combined_meta_prefilter %>%
  dplyr::filter(percent.MT<=5) %>%  # Filter out extreme outliers for better visualization)
  ggplot(aes(x = percent.MT, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_MT, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = paste0("Mitochondrial % (pre-filter) | threshold = ", QC_MAX_MT, "%"),
       x = "% Mitochondrial reads", y = "Density")
print(p_mt_dist)
Code 
plots_prefilter[["p_mt_dist"]] <- p_mt_dist

rm(p_mt_dist)

3.1.1.10 UMIs vs. genes detected (coloured by MT%)

Two metrics that are often evaluated together are the number of UMIs and the number of genes detected per cell. Here, we have plotted the number of genes versus the numnber of UMIs coloured by the fraction of mitochondrial reads. Mitochondrial read fractions are only high (light blue color) in particularly low count cells with few detected genes. This could be indicative of damaged/dying cells whose cytoplasmic mRNA has leaked out through a broken membrane, and thus, only mRNA located in the mitochondria is still conserved. These cells are filtered out by our count and gene number thresholds. Jointly visualizing the count and gene thresholds shows the joint filtering effect.

Cells that are poor quality are likely to have low genes and UMIs per cell, and correspond to the data points in the bottom left quadrant of the plot. Good cells will generally exhibit both higher number of genes per cell and higher numbers of UMIs.

With this plot we also evaluate the slope of the line, and any scatter of data points in the bottom right hand quadrant of the plot. These cells have a high number of UMIs but only a few number of genes. These could be dying cells, but also could represent a population of a low complexity celltype (i.e red blood cells).

Code 
p_umi_vs_genes <- combined_meta_prefilter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nCount_RNA, y = nFeature_RNA, color = percent.MT)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "grey90", high = "red") +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = QC_MIN_FEATURES, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 9) +
  labs(title = "UMIs vs Genes per cell (pre-filter, 50k sample)",
       x = "nCount_RNA (log10)", y = "nFeature_RNA (log10)")
print(p_umi_vs_genes)
Code 
plots_prefilter[["p_umi_vs_genes"]] <- p_umi_vs_genes

rm(p_umi_vs_genes)

3.1.1.11 Complexity (log10 Genes per UMI)

We can see the samples where we sequenced each cell less have a higher overall complexity, that is because we have not started saturating the sequencing for any given gene for these samples. Outlier cells in these samples might be cells that have a less complex RNA species than other cells. Sometimes we can detect contamination with low complexity cell types like red blood cells via this metric. Generally, we expect the novelty score to be above 0.80.

Code 
p_complexity <- combined_meta_prefilter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = log10GenesPerUMI, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COMPLEXITY, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Transcriptome complexity (pre-filter)",
       x = "log10(Genes / UMI)", y = "Density")

print(p_complexity)
Code 
plots_prefilter[["p_complexity"]] <- p_complexity

rm(p_complexity)
Code 
base::saveRDS(plots_prefilter, file.path(output_path, "RData", "QC_plots_prefilter.rds"))
plots_prefilter <- readRDS(file.path(output_path,"RData/QC_plots_prefilter.rds"))
rm(combined_meta_prefilter)
gc()
           used (Mb) gc trigger (Mb) max used (Mb)
Ncells 17784523  950   28869927 1542 21659535 1157
Vcells 83109890  634  118801296  906 85067216  649

3.2 Cell Filtering

Three-layer filtering strategy:

  1. Demuxlet singlet filter — Remove barcodes classified as doublets by Demuxlet (included in paper metadata: DemuxletType == "SNG")
  2. Study inclusion filter — Keep only cells the authors included (IncludedInStudy == TRUE)
  3. QC thresholds — Standard human PBMC thresholds
Code 
cat("Filtering Parameters:\n")
Filtering Parameters:
Code 
cat("===========================\n")
===========================
Code 
cat("Genes per cell: Minimun: ", QC_MIN_FEATURES,  ", Maximum: ", QC_MAX_FEATURES, "\n")
Genes per cell: Minimun:  500 , Maximum:  6000
Code 
cat("Genes Counts: Minimun: ", QC_MIN_COUNTS,  ", Maximum: ", QC_MAX_COUNTS, "\n")
Genes Counts: Minimun:  1000 , Maximum:  50000
Code 
cat("Ribosomal % Counts: Minimun: ", QC_MIN_RIBO,  ", Maximum: ", QC_MAX_RIBO, "\n")
Ribosomal % Counts: Minimun:  0 , Maximum:  60
Code 
cat("Maximum % mitochondrial reads:", QC_MAX_MT,"\n\n" )
Maximum % mitochondrial reads: 0.2
Code 
if (check_checkpoint("04_seurat_filtered")) {
  seurat_filtered <- load_checkpoint("04_seurat_filtered")
} else {
  libs_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData/filtered_libs/")
  dir.create(libs_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

  seurat_qc  <- load_checkpoint("02_seurat_qc")
  lib_names  <- names(seurat_qc)

  # --- Sequential loop: filter one library at a time --------------------------
  # After filtering each library it is saved individually and its slot in
  # seurat_qc is nulled so peak RAM stays bounded (~size of seurat_qc alone).
  for (lib_name in lib_names) {
    lib_rds <- paste0(libs_dir, "filtered_", lib_name, ".rds")

    if (file.exists(lib_rds)) {
      message(sprintf("[SKIP] Already filtered: %s", lib_name))
      seurat_qc[[lib_name]] <- NULL   # free slot even when skipping
      gc()
      next
    }

    obj <- seurat_qc[[lib_name]]
    n0  <- ncol(obj)
    
    # Layer 1: Demuxlet singlet filter
    

    # Layer 2: Paper inclusion filter
    # NOTE: cells with IncludedInStudy == FALSE may still pass QC;
    #        we include them here but flag them for optional downstream exclusion
    # (keep all singlets regardless of IncludedInStudy for our own QC analysis)

    # Layer 3: Standard human PBMC QC thresholds
    obj <- subset(obj,
                  subset = nFeature_RNA     >= QC_MIN_FEATURES   &
                           nFeature_RNA     <= QC_MAX_FEATURES   &
                           nCount_RNA       >= QC_MIN_COUNTS     &
                           nCount_RNA       <= QC_MAX_COUNTS     &
                           percent.MT       <= QC_MAX_MT         &
                           log10GenesPerUMI >= QC_MIN_COMPLEXITY)
    n1 <- ncol(obj)

    message(sprintf("[FILTER] %s: %d → filter %d (kept %.1f%%)",
                    lib_name, n0, n1,  n1 / n0 * 100))

    saveRDS(obj, file = lib_rds)
    message(sprintf("[SAVED]  %s", lib_rds))

    # Free this library's slot before loading the next one
    seurat_qc[[lib_name]] <- NULL
    rm(obj)
    gc()
  }
  rm(seurat_qc)
  gc()

  # --- Assemble final list from individual checkpoints ------------------------
  message("\n[ASSEMBLE] Loading individual filtered libraries...")
  seurat_filtered <- lapply(lib_names, function(lib_name) {
    readRDS(paste0(libs_dir, "filtered_", lib_name, ".rds"))
  })
  names(seurat_filtered) <- lib_names

  save_checkpoint(seurat_filtered, "04_seurat_filtered")

  # To remove individual per-library files after the combined checkpoint is
  # confirmed good, uncomment and run manually:
  # unlink(paste0(libs_dir, "filtered_", lib_names, ".rds"))
}
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_04_seurat_filtered.rds  (19:07:38)

3.2.1 Post-filter QC summary

Code 
# Quick check of matching quality (cells found in metadata)
import_filtered <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(seurat_filtered), function(nm) {
  obj <- seurat_filtered[[nm]]
  n_total     <- ncol(obj)
  data.frame(Library = nm, N_Cells = n_total,
             row.names = NULL)
}))

resume_filtered <- left_join(import_tbl, import_filtered, by = "Library") %>%
  dplyr::rename(N_Cells_PreFilter = N_Cells.x,
                N_Cells_PostFilter = N_Cells.y) %>%
  mutate(Retained.pct = N_Cells_PostFilter/N_Cells_PreFilter*100)

print(resume_filtered)

3.2.2.7 UMI counts distribution

The UMI counts per cell (nCount_RNA) should generally be above 500, that is the low end of what we expect. If UMI counts are between 500-1000 counts, it is usable but the cells probably should have been sequenced more deeply.

Code 
p_UMI_dist <- combined_meta_filter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nCount_RNA, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "UMI distribution per library (filter)",
       x = "nCount_RNA (log10)", y = "Density")

print(p_UMI_dist)
Code 
plots_filter[["p_UMI_dist"]] <- p_UMI_dist

rm(p_UMI_dist)

3.2.2.8 Genes detected per cell

We have similar expectations for gene detection as for UMI detection, although it may be a bit lower than UMIs. For high quality data, the proportional histogram should contain a single large peak that represents cells that were encapsulated. If we see a small shoulder to the right of the major peak (not present in our data), or a bimodal distribution of the cells, that can indicate a couple of things. It might be that there are a set of cells that failed for some reason. It could also be that there are biologically different types of cells (i.e. quiescent cell populations, less complex cells of interest), and/or one type is much smaller than the other (i.e. cells with high counts may be cells that are larger in size). Therefore, this threshold should be assessed with other metrics that we describe in this lesson.

Code 
p_genes_dist <-  combined_meta_filter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nFeature_RNA, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of number of genes (filter)",
       x = "nFeature_RNA", y = "Density")


print(p_genes_dist)
Code 
plots_filter[["p_genes_dist"]] <- p_genes_dist

rm(p_genes_dist)

3.2.2.9 Mitochondrial percentage

This metric can identify whether there is a large amount of mitochondrial contamination from dead or dying cells. We define poor quality samples for mitochondrial counts as cells which surpass the 0.2 mitochondrial ratio mark, unless of course you are expecting this in your sample.

Code 
p_mt_dist <- combined_meta_filter %>%
  dplyr::filter(percent.MT<=5) %>%  # Filter out extreme outliers for better visualization)
  ggplot(aes(x = percent.MT, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MAX_MT, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = paste0("Mitochondrial % (filter) | threshold = ", QC_MAX_MT, "%"),
       x = "% Mitochondrial reads", y = "Density")
print(p_mt_dist)
Code 
plots_filter[["p_mt_dist"]] <- p_mt_dist

rm(p_mt_dist)

3.2.2.10 UMIs vs. genes detected (coloured by MT%)

Two metrics that are often evaluated together are the number of UMIs and the number of genes detected per cell. Here, we have plotted the number of genes versus the numnber of UMIs coloured by the fraction of mitochondrial reads. Mitochondrial read fractions are only high (light blue color) in particularly low count cells with few detected genes. This could be indicative of damaged/dying cells whose cytoplasmic mRNA has leaked out through a broken membrane, and thus, only mRNA located in the mitochondria is still conserved. These cells are filtered out by our count and gene number thresholds. Jointly visualizing the count and gene thresholds shows the joint filtering effect.

Cells that are poor quality are likely to have low genes and UMIs per cell, and correspond to the data points in the bottom left quadrant of the plot. Good cells will generally exhibit both higher number of genes per cell and higher numbers of UMIs.

With this plot we also evaluate the slope of the line, and any scatter of data points in the bottom right hand quadrant of the plot. These cells have a high number of UMIs but only a few number of genes. These could be dying cells, but also could represent a population of a low complexity celltype (i.e red blood cells).

Code 
p_umi_vs_genes <- combined_meta_filter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = nCount_RNA, y = nFeature_RNA, color = percent.MT)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "grey90", high = "red") +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COUNTS, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = QC_MIN_FEATURES, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 9) +
  labs(title = "UMIs vs Genes per cell (filter, 50k sample)",
       x = "nCount_RNA (log10)", y = "nFeature_RNA (log10)")
print(p_umi_vs_genes)
Code 
plots_filter[["p_umi_vs_genes"]] <- p_umi_vs_genes

rm(p_umi_vs_genes)

3.2.2.11 Complexity (log10 Genes per UMI)

We can see the samples where we sequenced each cell less have a higher overall complexity, that is because we have not started saturating the sequencing for any given gene for these samples. Outlier cells in these samples might be cells that have a less complex RNA species than other cells. Sometimes we can detect contamination with low complexity cell types like red blood cells via this metric. Generally, we expect the novelty score to be above 0.80.

Code 
p_complexity <- combined_meta_filter %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = log10GenesPerUMI, color = library, fill = library)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = QC_MIN_COMPLEXITY, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Transcriptome complexity (filter)",
       x = "log10(Genes / UMI)", y = "Density")

print(p_complexity)
Code 
plots_filter[["p_complexity"]] <- p_complexity

rm(p_complexity)

plots_filter <- readRDS(file.path(output_path,"RData/QC_plots_filter.rds"))

Ncells 16702059  892   28869927 1542  28869927 1542
Vcells 43636693  333  137183159 1047 171478948 1308

4 Doublet Detection (DoubletFinder)

DoubletFinder is run per library (before integration) on QC-filtered cells. Each result is checkpointed. The Demuxlet classification already filtered inter-sample doublets; DoubletFinder catches intra-sample technical doublets.

Memory note: Processing runs sequentially (1 library at a time) to stay within 16 GB RAM. Each library is unloaded after processing.

Code 
message("[DOUBLETS] Running scDblFinder per sample...")
[DOUBLETS] Running scDblFinder per sample...
Code 
# Run DoubletFinder on each library (checkpoint per library)
#df_results <- lapply(names(seurat_filtered), function(lib_name) {
#  run_doubletfinder_bp(seurat_filtered[[lib_name]], lib_name, output_path)
#})
#names(df_results) <- names(seurat_filtered)

# Run DoubletFinder on each library (checkpoint per library). Using BPCells matrixs for faster processing.
  df_results  <- lapply(names(seurat_filtered), function(nm)
                   run_doubletfinder_bp(seurat_filtered[[nm]], nm, output_path))
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Ctrl_forebrain_1
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Ctrl_forebrain_2
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Ctrl_forebrain_3
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Inulin_forebrain_1
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Inulin_forebrain_2
[DF] Checkpoint found — loading: Inulin_forebrain_3
Code 
  names(df_results) <- names(seurat_filtered)

  seurat_singlets <- build_seurat_singlets(seurat_filtered, df_results) # Save singlet-only Seurat objects for downstream use
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_06_seurat_singlets.rds  (19:10:44)
Code 
  # Save "06_seurat_singlets.rds" checkpoint for downstream use
  rm(seurat_filtered, df_results)
  gc()
           used (Mb) gc trigger (Mb)  max used (Mb)
Ncells 16702854  892   28869927 1542  28869927 1542
Vcells 43677143  333  137183159 1047 171478948 1308

5 MERGE + NORMALISE + VARIABLE FEATURES + SCALE + PCA

Code 
singlets_path <- file.path(
  output_path, "RData",
  paste0(CHECKPOINT_PREFIX, "06_seurat_singlets.rds")
)
if (!file.exists(singlets_path)) {
  stop(
    "[ABORT] checkpoint_06_seurat_singlets.rds not found at: ", singlets_path,
    "\n  Run QC + doublet removal steps first."
  )
}

log_msg("[LOAD] Loading checkpoint_06_seurat_singlets from: ", singlets_path)
[19:10:46] [LOAD] Loading checkpoint_06_seurat_singlets from: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_06_seurat_singlets.rds
Code 
seurat_singlets <- readRDS(singlets_path)
sample_names <- names(seurat_singlets)
log_msg("[LOAD] ", length(sample_names), " samples: ", paste(sample_names, collapse = ", "))
[19:10:47] [LOAD] 6 samples: Ctrl_forebrain_1, Ctrl_forebrain_2, Ctrl_forebrain_3, Inulin_forebrain_1, Inulin_forebrain_2, Inulin_forebrain_3
Code 
log_msg("[RAM]  After load: ", ram_mb(), " MB")
[19:10:47] [RAM]  After load: 1225 MB
Code 
log_msg("[INFO]  output_path = ", output_path)
[19:10:48] [INFO]  output_path = output/GSE262881/LogNorm
Code 
log_msg("[INFO]  N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES = ", N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES)
[19:10:48] [INFO]  N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES = 2000
Code 
log_msg("[INFO]  N_PCS_INTEGRATION = ", N_PCS_INTEGRATION)
[19:10:48] [INFO]  N_PCS_INTEGRATION = 20

Merge, Normalization, Variable Feature Selection, Scaling, and PCA are all performed in a single step to minimize RAM usage. Seurat v5’s handling of split layers allows us to avoid iterative merging and the associated “.1”/“.2” suffix accumulation, while still applying normalization independently per sample.

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("lognorm_07_merged")) {
  log_msg("[SKIP] lognorm_07_merged found — loading...")
  merged <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_07_merged")
} else {
  # --- Merge (single call) ----------------------------------------------------
  # Single merge() avoids ".1"/".2" suffix accumulation from iterative merging.
  # RNA assay stays Assay5 + BPCells on disk throughout.
  log_msg("[MERGE] Single merge call — ", length(sample_names), " objects...")
  merged <- merge(
    seurat_singlets[[1]],
    y = seurat_singlets[-1]
  )
  rm(seurat_singlets)
  gc()

  log_msg(sprintf(
    "[MERGE] Done: %d cells | %d genes | RAM: %d MB",
    ncol(merged), nrow(merged), ram_mb()
  ))

  
# --- Normalise (per-layer, streaming over BPCells) --------------------------
  # Seurat v5 detects split layers and applies log-normalisation independently
  # per sample — equivalent to normalising each sample separately before merging.
  log_msg("[NORM] NormalizeData (LogNormalize, scale.factor = 10000)...")
  merged <- NormalizeData(merged,
    normalization.method = "LogNormalize",
    scale.factor         = 10000,
    verbose              = FALSE
  )
  log_msg("[RAM]  After NormalizeData: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- Variable features (consensus across samples) ---------------------------
  # FindVariableFeatures on split-layer objects computes HVGs per sample and
  # returns a consensus ranked set. Avoids one sample dominating HVG selection.
  log_msg("[HVG] FindVariableFeatures (nfeatures = ", N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES, ")...")
  merged <- FindVariableFeatures(merged,
    selection.method = "vst",
    nfeatures        = N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES,
    verbose          = FALSE
  )
  log_msg("[HVG] ", length(VariableFeatures(merged)), " variable features selected.")
  log_msg("[RAM]  After FindVariableFeatures: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- Scale (variable genes only, loads into RAM) ----------------------------
  # Only ~N_INTEGRATION_FEATURES genes are scaled — manageable RAM cost.
  # Required before PCA because NormalizeData does not centre or scale variance.
  log_msg("[SCALE] ScaleData on variable features...")
  merged <- ScaleData(merged,
    features = VariableFeatures(merged),
    verbose  = FALSE
  )
  log_msg("[RAM]  After ScaleData: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- PCA --------------------------------------------------------------------
  log_msg("[PCA] RunPCA (npcs = ", N_PCS_MAX, ")...")
  merged <- RunPCA(merged,
    assay   = "RNA",
    npcs    = N_PCS_MAX,
    verbose = FALSE
  )
  log_msg("[RAM]  After RunPCA: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # Variance explained by PCs (for elbow plot)
  pca_var    <- Stdev(merged, reduction = "pca")^2
  total_var  <- sum(pca_var)
  var_table  <- data.frame(
    PC                = seq_along(pca_var),
    Variance          = round(pca_var, 4),
    ExplainedVariance = round(pca_var / total_var * 100, 2),
    CumulativeVar     = round(cumsum(pca_var / total_var * 100), 2)
)
# Print first 30 PCs
print(head(var_table, 50))
writexl::write_xlsx(var_table, paste0(output_path, "/tables/Variance_by_PC.xlsx"))
  
  # Calculate Number of PC.
  pcs <- n_pcs(merged)
  #Add to merged object metadata
  merged@misc$pca_selection <- list(n_pcs = pcs, method = "elbow_cumulative")
  
  log_msg("[Number of PC]:", pcs)
  
  # Elbow plot to guide choice of N_PCS_INTEGRATION
  p_elbow <- ElbowPlot(merged, ndims = N_PCS_MAX) +
     geom_vline(xintercept = pcs, linetype = "dashed", color = "red")
  
  save_plot("lognorm_elbow", p_elbow, width = 8, height = 5, subdir = "integration/")
print(p_elbow)
  save_checkpoint(merged, "lognorm_07_merged")
  log_msg("[CHECKPOINT] lognorm_07_merged saved.")
}
[19:10:48] [SKIP] lognorm_07_merged found — loading...
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_lognorm_07_merged.rds  (19:10:48)
Code 
rm(seurat_singlets)

6 Integration

Integration is performed separately for RPCA and Harmony methods to allow comparison. Both methods start from the same merged object (pre-joined layers) to ensure a fair comparison. The RPCA integration is performed first, followed by Harmony, with separate checkpoints for each.

6.1 RPCA INTEGRATION

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("lognorm_08_rpca")) {
  log_msg("[SKIP] lognorm_08_rpca found — loading...")
  obj_rpca <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_08_rpca")
} else {
  log_msg("[RPCA] IntegrateLayers — method = RPCAIntegration...")
  merged <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_07_merged") # Start from merged object (pre-joined layers)
  n_pcs <- merged@misc$pca_selection$n_pcs
  log_msg("[INFO]  group.by.vars = sample_id | dims = 1:", n_pcs)

  obj_rpca <- IntegrateLayers(
    object               = merged,
    method               = RPCAIntegration,
    orig.reduction       = "pca",
    normalization.method = "LogNormalize",
    dims                 = 1:n_pcs,
    verbose              = TRUE
  )
  gc()
  log_msg("[RAM]  After RPCA integration: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- Join layers (required before FindMarkers and most visualisation) -------
  log_msg("[LAYERS] JoinLayers on RNA assay...")
  obj_rpca[["RNA"]] <- JoinLayers(obj_rpca[["RNA"]])
  gc()
  log_msg("[RAM]  After JoinLayers: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- Clustering + UMAP (RPCA) -----------------------------------------------
  log_msg("[CLUSTER] FindNeighbors + FindClusters (RPCA)...")
  obj_rpca <- FindNeighbors(obj_rpca,
    reduction = "integrated.dr",
    dims      = 1:n_pcs,
    verbose   = FALSE
  )

  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    obj_rpca <- FindClusters(obj_rpca,
      resolution = res,
      algorithm  = 1,
      verbose    = FALSE
    )
    log_msg(sprintf(
      "[CLUSTER] resolution %.2f → %d clusters",
      res, length(unique(obj_rpca@meta.data[[paste0("RNA_snn_res.", res)]]))
    ))
  }

  log_msg("[UMAP] RunUMAP on integrated.rpca (dims = 1:", n_pcs, ")...")
  obj_rpca <- RunUMAP(obj_rpca,
    reduction      = "integrated.dr",
    dims           = 1:n_pcs,
    umap.method    = "uwot",
    reduction.name = "umap.rpca",
    verbose        = FALSE
  )

  # Plots per resolution
  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    col_nm <- paste0("RNA_snn_res.", res)
    Idents(obj_rpca) <- col_nm
    p <- DimPlot(obj_rpca,
      reduction = "umap.rpca",
      label     = TRUE,
      repel     = TRUE,
      raster = FALSE
    ) +
      ggtitle(paste0("RPCA | resolution ", res))
    save_plot(
      paste0("lognorm_rpca_umap_res", gsub("\\.", "", as.character(res))),
      p,
      width = 10, height = 8, subdir = "clustering"
    )
  }

  p_sample <- DimPlot(obj_rpca,
    reduction  = "umap.rpca",
    group.by   = "sample_id",
    label      = FALSE) +
    ggtitle("RPCA | by sample")
  
  save_plot("lognorm_rpca_umap_sample", 
            p_sample,
            width = 10, height = 8,
            subdir = "clustering/")
  
  #print(p_sample)
  
  for (dims_range in list(1:6, 7:12, 13:18)) {
  DimHeatmap(obj_rpca, dims = dims_range, cells = 500, balanced = TRUE)
  }
  
  
  save_checkpoint(obj_rpca, "lognorm_08_rpca")
  log_msg("[CHECKPOINT] lognorm_08_rpca saved.")
  log_msg("[RAM]  Final RPCA block: ", ram_mb(), " MB")
}
[19:10:51] [SKIP] lognorm_08_rpca found — loading...
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_lognorm_08_rpca.rds  (19:10:51)

6.1.1 Clustering Resolution Sweep

Tests combinations of PCs (10, 20, 30) × resolutions (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8) to find the optimal clustering. Results visualized with clustree.

Memory note: Each combination saves its .rds file individually. Already-computed combinations are skipped on re-run.

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("RPCA_cluster_sweep_metadata")) {
  combined_metadata <- load_checkpoint("RPCA_cluster_sweep_metadata")
  message("[CLUSTERING] Loaded sweep results from checkpoint.")
} else {
  obj_rpca <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_08_rpca") # Ensure obj_rpca is in memory for clustering sweep
  combined_metadata <- data.frame()
  sweep_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData")

  for (dims in CLUSTERING_DIMS) {
    for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
      sweep_name <- paste0("RPCA_Dims_", dims, "_Res_", res)
      rds_file   <- paste0(sweep_dir, "/sweep_", sweep_name, ".rds")

      if (file.exists(rds_file)) {
        message(sprintf("[SWEEP] Loading cached: dims=%d res=%.1f", dims, res))
        tmp <- readRDS(rds_file)
      } else {
        message(sprintf("[SWEEP] Computing: dims=%d res=%.1f  [%s]",
                        dims, res, format(Sys.time(), "%H:%M:%S")))
        tic()
        tmp <- FindNeighbors(obj_rpca, dims = 1:dims, verbose = FALSE)
        tmp <- FindClusters(tmp,  resolution = res,  verbose = FALSE)
        tmp <- RunUMAP(tmp, dims = 1:dims, verbose = FALSE)

        # Save UMAP plot
        p <- DimPlot(tmp, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, raster = FALSE) +
          ggtitle(paste("dims =", dims, "| res =", res)) + NoLegend()
        print(p)
        save_plot(paste0("UMAP_sweep_", sweep_name), p, subdir = "clustering")

        saveRDS(tmp, file = rds_file)
        toc()
        gc()
      }

      # Collect cluster assignments for clustree
      cell_df <- data.frame(
        cell       = colnames(tmp),
        cluster    = as.character(Idents(tmp)),
        dims       = as.character(dims),
        resolution = as.character(res),
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
      combined_metadata <- dplyr::bind_rows(combined_metadata, cell_df)
      rm(tmp)
      gc()
    }
  }

  save_checkpoint(combined_metadata, "RPCA_cluster_sweep_metadata")
}
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_RPCA_cluster_sweep_metadata.rds  (19:10:54)
[CLUSTERING] Loaded sweep results from checkpoint.

6.1.2 Clustree

Visualizes cluster stability across resolutions for each dimension setting.

Code 
  for (d in CLUSTERING_DIMS) {
    subset_meta <- combined_metadata %>%
      dplyr::filter(dims == as.character(d)) %>%
      tidyr::pivot_wider(
        id_cols     = cell,
        names_from  = resolution,
        values_from = cluster,
        names_prefix = "Res_"
      ) 
      
    p_tree <- clustree::clustree(subset_meta, prefix = "Res_") +
      ggtitle(paste("Clustree — RPCA — PCs =", d))
    print(p_tree)
    save_plot(paste0("Clustree_RPCA_dims", d), p_tree,
              width = 10, height = 12, subdir = "clustering")
  }

6.1.3 Cell Type Proportion

Tabulate cluster proportions by condition for every dims × resolution combination in the RPCA sweep.

Code 
proportion_results <- list()
sweep_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData")

for (dims in CLUSTERING_DIMS) {
  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    sweep_name <- paste0("RPCA_Dims_", dims, "_Res_", res)
    rds_file   <- file.path(sweep_dir, paste0("sweep_", sweep_name, ".rds"))

    if (!file.exists(rds_file)) {
      message(sprintf("[PROPORTION] Skipping %s — file not found", sweep_name))
      next
    }

    tmp <- readRDS(rds_file)
    proportions <- as.data.frame(prop.table(table(Idents(tmp), tmp$condition), margin = 2))
    proportions$resolution <- as.character(res)
    proportions$dims       <- as.character(dims)
    proportion_results[[sweep_name]] <- proportions
    rm(tmp); gc()
  }
}

proportions_all <- do.call(rbind, proportion_results)
proportions_all %>%
  write_xlsx(file.path(output_path, "tables", "Cell_type_proportion_RPCA.xlsx"))

proportions_all$dims <- factor(proportions_all$dims, levels = unique(proportions_all$dims))
proportions_all$resolution <- factor(proportions_all$resolution, levels = unique(proportions_all$resolution))


for (res in levels(proportions_all$resolution)) {
  filtered_data <- proportions_all %>% dplyr::filter(resolution == res)
  
  
  p <- ggplot(filtered_data) +
    aes(x = Var2, y = Freq, fill = Var1) +
    geom_bar(stat = "summary", fun = "sum") +
    scale_fill_hue(direction = 1) +
    labs(
      x = "Cluster", y = "Proportion", fill = "Condition",
      title = paste("RPCA — Cluster Proportions | Resolution =", res)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    facet_wrap(vars(dims), labeller = labeller(dims = \(x) paste0("PCs = ", x))) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray", linetype = "dotted"),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
  
  
  save_plot(paste0("Cell_Proportion_RPCA_resolution_", res), p, subdir = "clustering")
  print(p)
}

6.2 HARMONY INTEGRATION

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("lognorm_09_harmony")) {
  log_msg("[SKIP] lognorm_09_harmony found — nothing to do.")
} else {
  log_msg("[HARMONY] IntegrateLayers — method = HarmonyIntegration...")
  merged <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_07_merged") # Start from merged object (pre-joined layers)
  n_pcs <- merged@misc$pca_selection$n_pcs
  log_msg("[INFO]  group.by.vars = sample_id | dims = 1:", n_pcs)
  

  # Work from the pre-joined merged object so Harmony gets the split layers
  # (required for IntegrateLayers — JoinLayers must not be done beforehand).
  obj_harmony <- IntegrateLayers(
    object               = merged,
    method               = HarmonyIntegration,
    orig.reduction       = "pca",
    group.by.vars        = "sample_id",
    normalization.method = "LogNormalize",
    dims                 = 1:n_pcs,
    verbose              = TRUE
  )
  gc()
  log_msg("[RAM]  After Harmony integration: ", ram_mb(), " MB")

  # --- Join layers ------------------------------------------------------------
  log_msg("[LAYERS] JoinLayers on RNA assay...")
  obj_harmony[["RNA"]] <- JoinLayers(obj_harmony[["RNA"]])
  gc()

  # --- Clustering + UMAP (Harmony) --------------------------------------------
  log_msg("[CLUSTER] FindNeighbors + FindClusters (Harmony)...")
  obj_harmony <- FindNeighbors(obj_harmony,
    reduction = "harmony",
    dims      = 1:n_pcs,
    verbose   = FALSE
  )

  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    obj_harmony <- FindClusters(obj_harmony,
      resolution = res,
      algorithm  = 1,
      verbose    = FALSE
    )
    log_msg(sprintf(
      "[CLUSTER] resolution %.2f → %d clusters",
      res, length(unique(obj_harmony@meta.data[[paste0("RNA_snn_res.", res)]]))
    ))
  }

  log_msg("[UMAP] RunUMAP on harmony (dims = 1:", n_pcs, ")...")
  obj_harmony <- RunUMAP(obj_harmony,
    reduction      = "harmony",
    dims           = 1:n_pcs,
    umap.method    = "uwot",
    reduction.name = "umap.harmony",
    verbose        = FALSE
  )

  # Plots per resolution
  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    col_nm <- paste0("RNA_snn_res.", res)
    Idents(obj_harmony) <- col_nm
    p <- DimPlot(obj_harmony,
      reduction = "umap.harmony",
      label     = TRUE,
      repel     = TRUE,
      raster = FALSE
    ) +
      ggtitle(paste0("Harmony | resolution ", res))
    save_plot(
      paste0("lognorm_harmony_umap_res", gsub("\\.", "", as.character(res))),
      p,
      width = 10, height = 8, subdir = "clustering"
    )
  }

  p_sample <- DimPlot(obj_harmony,
    reduction  = "umap.harmony",
    group.by   = "sample_id",
    label      = FALSE,
    raster = FALSE) +
    ggtitle("Harmony | by sample")
  
 #print(p_sample)
  
  save_plot("lognorm_harmony_umap_sample", 
            p_sample,
            width = 10, height = 8,
            subdir = "clustering")

  for (dims_range in list(1:6, 7:12, 13:18)) {
  DimHeatmap(obj_harmony, dims = dims_range, cells = 500, balanced = TRUE)
}
  
  save_checkpoint(obj_harmony, "lognorm_09_harmony")
  log_msg("[CHECKPOINT] lognorm_03_harmony saved.")
  log_msg("[RAM]  Final Harmony block: ", ram_mb(), " MB")
}

6.2.1 Clustering Resolution Sweep

Tests combinations of PCs (10, 20, 30) × resolutions (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8) to find the optimal clustering. Results visualized with clustree.

Memory note: Each combination saves its .rds file individually. Already-computed combinations are skipped on re-run.

Code 
if (check_checkpoint("Harmony_cluster_sweep_metadata")) {
  combined_metadata <- load_checkpoint("Harmony_cluster_sweep_metadata")
  message("[CLUSTERING] Loaded sweep results from checkpoint.")
} else {
  obj_harmony <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_09_harmony") # Ensure obj_rpca is in memory for clustering sweep
  combined_metadata <- data.frame()
  sweep_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData")

  for (dims in CLUSTERING_DIMS) {
    for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
      sweep_name <- paste0("Harmony_Dims_", dims, "_Res_", res)
      rds_file   <- paste0(sweep_dir, "/sweep_", sweep_name, ".rds")

      if (file.exists(rds_file)) {
        message(sprintf("[SWEEP] Loading cached: dims=%d res=%.1f", dims, res))
        tmp <- readRDS(rds_file)
      } else {
        message(sprintf("[SWEEP] Computing: dims=%d res=%.1f  [%s]",
                        dims, res, format(Sys.time(), "%H:%M:%S")))
        tic()
        tmp <- FindNeighbors(obj_harmony, dims = 1:dims, verbose = FALSE)
        tmp <- FindClusters(tmp,  resolution = res,  verbose = FALSE)
        tmp <- RunUMAP(tmp, dims = 1:dims, verbose = FALSE)

        # Save UMAP plot
        p <- DimPlot(tmp, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, raster = FALSE) +
          ggtitle(paste("dims =", dims, "| res =", res)) + NoLegend()
        print(p)
        save_plot(paste0("UMAP_sweep_", sweep_name), p, subdir = "clustering")

        saveRDS(tmp, file = rds_file)
        toc()
        gc()
      }

      # Collect cluster assignments for clustree
      cell_df <- data.frame(
        cell       = colnames(tmp),
        cluster    = as.character(Idents(tmp)),
        dims       = as.character(dims),
        resolution = as.character(res),
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      )
      combined_metadata <- dplyr::bind_rows(combined_metadata, cell_df)
      rm(tmp)
      gc()
    }
  }

  save_checkpoint(combined_metadata, "Harmony_cluster_sweep_metadata")
}

6.2.3 Cell Type Proportion

Tabulate cluster proportions by condition for every dims × resolution combination in the Harmony sweep.

Code 
proportion_results <- list()
sweep_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData")

for (dims in CLUSTERING_DIMS) {
  for (res in CLUSTERING_RESOLUTIONS) {
    sweep_name <- paste0("Harmony_Dims_", dims, "_Res_", res)
    rds_file   <- file.path(sweep_dir, paste0("sweep_", sweep_name, ".rds"))

    if (!file.exists(rds_file)) {
      message(sprintf("[PROPORTION] Skipping %s — file not found", sweep_name))
      next
    }

    tmp <- readRDS(rds_file)
    proportions <- as.data.frame(prop.table(table(Idents(tmp), tmp$condition), margin = 2))
    proportions$resolution <- as.character(res)
    proportions$dims       <- as.character(dims)
    proportion_results[[sweep_name]] <- proportions
    rm(tmp); gc()
  }
}

proportions_all <- do.call(rbind, proportion_results)
proportions_all %>%
  write_xlsx(file.path(output_path, "tables", "Cell_type_proportion_Harmony.xlsx"))

proportions_all$dims <- factor(proportions_all$dims, levels = unique(proportions_all$dims))
proportions_all$resolution <- factor(proportions_all$resolution, levels = unique(proportions_all$resolution))


for (res in levels(proportions_all$resolution)) {
  filtered_data <- proportions_all %>% dplyr::filter(resolution == res)
  
  
  p <- ggplot(filtered_data) +
    aes(x = Var2, y = Freq, fill = Var1) +
    geom_bar(stat = "summary", fun = "sum") +
    scale_fill_hue(direction = 1) +
    labs(
      x = "Cluster", y = "Proportion", fill = "Condition",
      title = paste("Harmony — Cluster Proportions | Resolution =", res)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    facet_wrap(vars(dims), labeller = labeller(dims = \(x) paste0("PCs = ", x))) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray", linetype = "dotted"),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
  save_plot(paste0("Cell_Proportion_Harmony_Resolution_", res), p, subdir = "clustering")
  print(p)
}

10 Finding differentially expressed features (cluster biomarkers)

Seurat can help you find markers that define clusters via differential expression (DE). By default, it identifies positive and negative markers of a single cluster (specified in ident.1), compared to all other cells. FindAllMarkers() automates this process for all clusters, but you can also test groups of clusters vs. each other, or against all cells.

In Seurat v5, we use the presto package (as described here and available for installation here), to dramatically improve the speed of DE analysis, particularly for large datasets. For users who are not using presto, you can examine the documentation for this function (?FindMarkers) to explore the min.pct and logfc.threshold parameters, which can be increased in order to increase the speed of DE testing.

FindMarkers loop — Condition comparison within each cluster

Purpose: Differential expression between Ctrl vs Inulin inside each cluster separately. This answers: “Which genes are dysregulated by the treatment in each specific cell type?”

Idents flow: 1. Idents(data_select) <- “seurat_clusters” — used only to subset() cells per cluster 2. Idents(cluster_cells) <- cluster_cells$condition — immediately overwritten to Ctrl/Inulin before FindMarkers

This is correct. ident.1 = “Ctrl” vs ident.2 = “Inulin” is unambiguous.

Output: one table per cluster with genes differentially expressed between conditions.

Key conceptual difference

┌────────────────┬────────────────────┬───────────────────────┐ │ │ FindMarkers (loop) │ FindAllMarkers │ ├────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤ │ Comparison │ Ctrl vs Inulin │ Cluster vs all others │ ├────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤ │ Question │ Treatment effect │ Cell type identity │ ├────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤ │ Idents at call │ condition │ seurat_clusters │ ├────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤ │ Use case │ DE analysis │ Cluster annotation │ └────────────────┴────────────────────┴───────────────────────┘

10.1 FindMarkers

Subset Clusters and Compare Genotypes

10.1.1 Convert matrix to BPCells

Code 
options(future.globals.maxSize =14000 * 1024^2) # 16 GB limit
plan("sequential") # Use 4 workers
gc()
           used (Mb) gc trigger (Mb)  max used (Mb)
Ncells 16941278  905   28869927 1542  28869927 1542
Vcells 59094998  451  131786990 1006 171478948 1308
Code 
# data_select inherits log-normalization from lognorm_07_merged via the Harmony sweep
DefaultAssay(data_select) <- "RNA"

# Unir las capas del assay RNA antes de ejecutar FindMarkers
data_select <- JoinLayers(data_select, assay = "RNA")

# BPCells stores joined matrices in column-major order; FindMarkers (wilcox) reads
# the `data` layer, so that is the one that must be row-major.
# Write to a permanent directory so checkpoints survive across R sessions
# (transpose_storage_order without outdir writes to /tmp, which is lost on restart).
bp_layers_dir <- file.path(output_path, "RData", "bpcells_layers")
dir.create(bp_layers_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
for (lyr in c("counts", "data")) {
  mat <- LayerData(data_select, assay = "RNA", layer = lyr)
  if (inherits(mat, "IterableMatrix")) {
    out_path <- file.path(bp_layers_dir, lyr)
    if (dir.exists(out_path)) unlink(out_path, recursive = TRUE)
    # "data" layer contains log-normalized floats; convert before writing so
    # BPCells uses float compression instead of integer (avoids the warning).
    if (lyr == "data") mat <- BPCells::convert_matrix_type(mat, type = "float")
    LayerData(data_select, assay = "RNA", layer = lyr) <-
      BPCells::transpose_storage_order(mat) |>
      BPCells::write_matrix_dir(out_path)
  }
}
Warning: Matrix compression performs poorly with non-integers.
• Consider calling convert_matrix_type if a compressed integer matrix is intended.
This message is displayed once every 8 hours.
Code 
rm(mat)

# Save the modified Seurat object with BPCells layers
save_checkpoint(data_select, "lognorm_10_bp_cells")

Also defined by 'alabaster.base'
Found more than one class "package_version" in cache; using the first, from namespace 'SeuratObject'
Also defined by 'alabaster.base'
[CHECKPOINT] Saved: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_lognorm_10_bp_cells.rds  (19:16:48)
Code 
# load checkpoint to verify
data_select_bp <- load_checkpoint("lognorm_10_bp_cells")
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_lognorm_10_bp_cells.rds  (19:16:48)
Code 
if (check_checkpoint("cluster_markers_by_condition")) {
  cluster_markers_by_condition <- load_checkpoint("cluster_markers_by_condition")
} else {

# Verificar que 'condition' está en metadata
if (!"condition" %in% colnames(data_select_bp@meta.data)) {
  stop("Error: Column 'condition' does not exist in metadata. Verify that 'Ctrl' & 'Inulin' are correctly assigned.")
}
# Establecer los clústeres como identidades antes de la iteración
Idents(data_select_bp) <- "seurat_clusters"  # Set cluster identities

# List to store results
cluster_list <- list()

# Loop through each cluster
cluster_ids <- levels(data_select_bp$seurat_clusters)

for (cluster in cluster_ids) {
  # Subset cells belonging to the current cluster
  cluster_cells <- subset(data_select_bp, idents = cluster)

  # Restore row-major order lost by subset() and write to disk so BPCells can
  # serve gene-wise rows without buffering the full column-major matrix in RAM.
  bp_subset_dir <- file.path(bp_layers_dir, "subset_tmp")
  for (lyr in c("counts", "data")) {
    mat <- LayerData(cluster_cells, assay = "RNA", layer = lyr)
    if (inherits(mat, "IterableMatrix")) {
      out_path <- file.path(bp_subset_dir, lyr)
      if (dir.exists(out_path)) unlink(out_path, recursive = TRUE)
      LayerData(cluster_cells, assay = "RNA", layer = lyr) <-
        BPCells::transpose_storage_order(mat) |>
        BPCells::write_matrix_dir(out_path)
    }
  }

  Idents(cluster_cells) <- cluster_cells$condition  # Asigna 'WT' y 'KO' directamente como identidades                                    # Verificar que el clúster tiene ambas condiciones antes de correr FindMarkers
  if (length(unique(Idents(cluster_cells))) < 2) {
    warning(paste("Skipping cluster", cluster, "because does not have cells in both conditions (Ctlr & Inulin)."))
    next  # Salta al siguiente clúster
  }                                 
  # Compare WT vs KO within the cluster
  res <- FindMarkers(cluster_cells, 
                     ident.1 = "Ctrl", 
                     ident.2 = "Inulin", 
                     test.use = "wilcox", # Cambia si prefieres otro test
                     assay = "RNA",          # Use RNA assay
                     min.pct = 0.1,         # Filter genes expressed in at least 25% of cells
                     logfc.threshold = 0.1) # Minimum log fold-change threshold
  
  # Add cluster info to results
  res$gene <- rownames(res)
  res$cluster <- cluster
  cluster_list[[cluster]] <- res
}

# Save using the name
save(cluster_list, file = paste0(output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_list", ".RData"))  
cluster_list %>% 
write_xlsx(paste0(output_path,"/tables/","cluster_list.xlsx"))
# Load results ####
load(paste0(output_path,"/RData/cluster_list.RData"))

# Convertir la lista en un data.frame con una columna adicional 'cluster' (valor numérico)
cluster_markers_by_condition <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(cluster_list), function(cluster) {
  # Extraer el dataframe del cluster actual y añadir una columna con el número del cluster
  markers <- cluster_list[[cluster]]
  markers$cluster <- as.numeric(gsub("Cluster_", "", cluster)) # Eliminar el prefijo y convertir a numérico
  return(markers)
}))

# Reiniciar los nombres de las filas
rownames(cluster_markers_by_condition) <- NULL




cluster_markers_by_condition <- cluster_markers_by_condition %>% 
  dplyr::select(gene, everything())

# Ver el resultado
head(cluster_markers_by_condition)

#Generate a list 

cluster_markers_by_condition %>% 
write_xlsx(paste0(output_path,"/tables/","cluster_markers_by_condition.xlsx"))
# Save using the name
save(cluster_markers_by_condition, file = paste0(output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_markers_by_condition", ".RData"))
  save_checkpoint(cluster_markers_by_condition, "cluster_markers_by_condition")

} # end FindMarkers checkpoint guard
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_cluster_markers_by_condition.rds  (19:16:50)

10.2 FindAllMarkers

FindAllMarkers — Cluster identity markers

Purpose: For every cluster, finds genes that distinguish it from all other clusters combined. This answers: “What genes define each cluster’s cell identity?” — the standard step for cell type annotation.

Idents: Idents(data_select_bp) <- “seurat_clusters” — correct. FindAllMarkers iterates each cluster as ident.1 and uses all remaining cells as ident.2 automatically.

Output: one combined table across all clusters with canonical marker genes per cluster.

Code 
plan("sequential")
options(future.globals.maxSize = 14000 * 1024^2)
gc()
           used (Mb) gc trigger (Mb)  max used (Mb)
Ncells 16946604  905   28869927 1542  28869927 1542
Vcells 76994391  588  131786990 1006 171478948 1308
Code 
if (check_checkpoint("cluster_markers_by_cluster")) {
  cluster_markers_by_cluster <- load_checkpoint("cluster_markers_by_cluster")
} else {

DefaultAssay(data_select_bp) <- "RNA"
Idents(data_select_bp) <- "seurat_clusters"

cluster_markers_by_cluster <- FindAllMarkers(data_select_bp,
                                      test.use = "wilcox",
                                      only.pos = FALSE,
                                      min.pct = 0.25,
                                      logfc.threshold = 0.1,
                                      max.cells.per.ident = 300)

cluster_markers_by_cluster %>%
write_xlsx(paste0(output_path,"/tables/","cluster_markers_by_cluster.xlsx"))
save(cluster_markers_by_cluster, file = paste0(output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_markers_by_cluster", ".RData"))
  save_checkpoint(cluster_markers_by_cluster, "cluster_markers_by_cluster")

} # end FindAllMarkers checkpoint guard

10.2.1 HeatMap

Code 
  DefaultAssay(data_select_bp) <- "RNA"

  # top10 genes markers per cluster
  top10_all <- cluster_markers_by_cluster %>%
    group_by(cluster) %>%
    dplyr::filter(avg_log2FC > FC, p_val_adj < 0.05) %>%
    slice_head(n = 10) %>%
    ungroup()

  # top10 genes markers by condition and cluster
  top10 <- cluster_markers_by_condition %>%
    group_by(cluster) %>%
    dplyr::filter(avg_log2FC > FC, p_val_adj < 0.05) %>%
    slice_head(n = 10) %>%
    ungroup()

  # Scale only the marker genes needed — avoids scaling all ~30k genes
  genes_to_scale <- unique(c(top10_all$gene, top10$gene))
  genes_to_scale <- genes_to_scale[genes_to_scale %in% rownames(data_select_bp)]
  data_select_bp <- ScaleData(data_select_bp, features = genes_to_scale, assay = "RNA")
Centering and scaling data matrix

scale.data

  print(plot)

save_plot("HeatMap_cluster_plot_by_cluster", plot2, height = 18)

11 Automatic Clustering association

  1. Use ‘SingleR’ is a widely used tool that compares gene expression profiles of your clusters to predefined reference databases. Load an appropriate reference database. For example:
  • MouseRNAseqData: Reference for murine tissues.
  • ImmGenData: Immunological reference.
  • HumanPrimaryCellAtlasData: For human cells.

  1. Use scType for fast annotation ‘scType’ is a simpler, more customizable tool that uses user-defined or database-available marker lists.

    Comparison between ‘SingleR’ and ‘scType’

    Recommendation If you have a well-characterized dataset (such as mouse brain transcriptomics), use SingleR with a database such as MouseRNAseqData. If you prefer greater flexibility or speed, try scType with specific marker lists.
    Feature SingleR scType
    Ease of use More robust but more technical Simpler
    Database Use predefined references Use marker lists
    Customization Limited Very customizable
    Speed Moderate Very fast

11.1 Allen Brain Atlas clustering

Download matrix Gene expression matrix and Table cell metadata from Allen Brain Atlas Cell Types Database: RNA-Seq Data (https://portal.brain-map.org/atlases-and-data/rnaseq) Whole Cortex & Hippocampus - 10x genomics (2020) with 10x-SMART-seq taxonomy (2021)

11.2 Generate Matrix from FindAllMarkers

Code 
# Extraer la matriz de expresión y las identidades de los clusters
#cluster.markers <- GetAssayData(data_select_bp, slot = "data")
#cluster.ids <- Idents(data_select_bp)
Code 
# Asegúrate de que el assay está configurado correctamente
DefaultAssay(data_select_bp) <- "RNA"

# Extraer los genes significativos encontrados por FindAllMarkers
significant_genes <- cluster_markers_by_cluster %>% 
  dplyr::filter(p_val_adj < 0.05) %>% 
  dplyr::select(gene) %>% 
  dplyr::distinct()%>% 
  dplyr::pull(gene)
  
# Extraer la matriz de expresión para esos genes (utilizando 'layer' en lugar de 'slot')
expression_matrix <- GetAssayData(data_select_bp, layer = "data")[significant_genes, ]

# BPCells devuelve un IterableMatrix; convertir a dgCMatrix (sparse) evita
# la materialización densa de 11+ GiB que provoca el crash de memoria.
if (inherits(expression_matrix, "IterableMatrix")) {
  expression_matrix <- as(expression_matrix, "dgCMatrix")
}

# Extraer las identidades de los clústeres
cluster.ids <- Idents(data_select_bp)

# Crear un objeto SummarizedExperiment con la matriz de expresión
cluster.markers <- SummarizedExperiment(
  assays = list(logcounts = expression_matrix),
  rowData = DataFrame(gene = rownames(expression_matrix)),
  colData = DataFrame(cluster = cluster.ids)
)

rm(expression_matrix, significant_genes)
gc()
            used (Mb) gc trigger  (Mb)   max used  (Mb)
Ncells  16727205  893   28869927  1542   28869927  1542
Vcells 720779486 5499 1877597742 14325 1877597742 14325

Code 
# Revisar dimensiones
dim(median_data)
[1] 31053   388
Code 
dim(trimmed_means_data)
[1] 31053   388
Code 
# Convertir a matriz
median_matrix <- as.matrix(median_data[ , -1, with = FALSE])  # Asumiendo que la primera columna son los nombres de genes
rownames(median_matrix) <- median_data[[1]]  # Asignar nombres de genes a las filas

trimmed_means_matrix <- as.matrix(trimmed_means_data[ , -1, with = FALSE])  # Similar al anterior
rownames(trimmed_means_matrix) <- trimmed_means_data[[1]]

# Crear referencia de SingleR
# Crear un nuevo objeto SummarizedExperiment con logcounts
reference_median <- SummarizedExperiment(
    assays = list(logcounts = log1p(median_matrix)), # Aplicar log-transformation
    rowData = DataFrame(gene = rownames(median_matrix)),
    colData = DataFrame(label.main = colnames(median_matrix))
)
# Revisar las etiquetas
colData(reference_median)
DataFrame with 387 rows and 1 column
                 label.main
                <character>
108_Pvalb         108_Pvalb
229_L6 IT CTX 229_L6 IT CTX
26_Ntng1 HPF   26_Ntng1 HPF
67_Sst               67_Sst
6_Lamp5 Lhx6   6_Lamp5 Lhx6
...                     ...
324_ProS           324_ProS
178_L4 IT CTX 178_L4 IT CTX
284_L6 CT CTX 284_L6 CT CTX
377_Astro         377_Astro
298_L6 CT ENT 298_L6 CT ENT
Code 
# Confirmar que `label.main` contiene los nombres de los tipos celulares
reference_median$label.main <- colnames(median_matrix)
Code 
# Comparar con la base de datos de referencia
annotations_median <- SingleR(test = cluster.markers, 
                       ref = reference_median, 
                       labels = reference_median$label.main,
                       clusters = cluster.ids)


# Revisar resultados
head(annotations_median)
DataFrame with 6 rows and 4 columns
                             scores           labels delta.next
                           <matrix>      <character>  <numeric>
0 0.0975733:0.0746616:0.0920308:...        374_Oligo  1.3595996
1 0.2655280:0.4516351:0.5236197:...  168_L2/3 IT CTX  0.0568386
2 0.0774290:0.0257786:0.0535424:...    386_Micro-PVM  0.0550191
3 0.2978010:0.4866855:0.5443031:... 161_L2/3 IT ENTl  0.2800968
4 0.3206043:0.5945461:0.6911577:...          100_Sst  0.0752106
5 0.2998185:0.4664490:0.5426738:...    288_L6 CT CTX  0.3569354
     pruned.labels
       <character>
0        374_Oligo
1  168_L2/3 IT CTX
2    386_Micro-PVM
3 161_L2/3 IT ENTl
4          100_Sst
5    288_L6 CT CTX

11.2.1.1 Plot Median

Code 
new.cluster.median <- annotations_median$labels
names(new.cluster.median) <- levels(data_select_bp)
data_select_bp_median <- RenameIdents(data_select_bp, new.cluster.median)

# Convertir las anotaciones a un data.frame para unirlas con cluster_markers_by_condition
annotations_median_df <- as.data.frame(annotations_median)
annotations_median_df$cluster <- rownames(annotations_median_df)  # Añadir los IDs de los clusters como columna

# Añadir columna 'labels'
cell_types_median <- setNames(annotations_median_df$labels, rownames(annotations_median_df))
cluster_markers_by_condition$labels_median <- cell_types_median[as.character(cluster_markers_by_condition$cluster)]
Code 
# Visualiza con las nuevas identidades
plot <- DimPlot(data_select_bp_median, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, repel = TRUE)
print(plot)
Code 
save_plot("UMAP_median_cluster_plot", plot, width = 12, height = 6)

11.2.2 Trimmed_Means

Code 
reference_trimmed <- SummarizedExperiment(
    assays = list(logcounts = log1p(trimmed_means_matrix)), # Aplicar log-transformation
    rowData = DataFrame(gene = rownames(trimmed_means_matrix)),
    colData = DataFrame(label.main = colnames(trimmed_means_matrix))
)
# Revisar las etiquetas
colData(reference_trimmed)

284_L6 CT CTX 284_L6 CT CTX
377_Astro         377_Astro
298_L6 CT ENT 298_L6 CT ENT
Code 
# Confirmar que `label.main` contiene los nombres de los tipos celulares
reference_trimmed$label.main <- colnames(trimmed_means_matrix)
Code 
# Comparar con la base de datos de referencia
annotations_trimmed <- SingleR(test = cluster.markers, 
                       ref = reference_trimmed, 
                       labels = reference_trimmed$label.main,
                       clusters = cluster.ids)


# Revisar resultados
head(annotations_trimmed)
DataFrame with 6 rows and 4 columns

11.2.2.1 Plot Trimmed

Code 
new.cluster.trimmed <- annotations_trimmed$labels
names(new.cluster.trimmed) <- levels(data_select_bp)
data_select_bp_trimmed <- RenameIdents(data_select_bp, new.cluster.trimmed)
# Convertir las anotaciones a un data.frame para unirlas con cluster_markers_by_condition
annotations_trimmed_df <- as.data.frame(annotations_trimmed)
annotations_trimmed_df$cluster <- rownames(annotations_trimmed_df)  # Añadir los IDs de los clusters como columna

# Añadir columna 'labels'
cell_types_trimmed <- setNames(annotations_trimmed_df$labels, rownames(annotations_trimmed_df))
cluster_markers_by_condition$labels_trimmed <- cell_types_trimmed[as.character(cluster_markers_by_condition$cluster)]
Code 
# Visualiza con las nuevas identidades
plot <- DimPlot(data_select_bp_trimmed, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, repel = TRUE)
print(plot)
Code 
save_plot("UMAP_median_trimmed_cluster_plot", plot, width = 12, height = 6)

11.3 MouseRNAseqData

Code 
ref <- MouseRNAseqData()  # Change according needs
# Extraer la matriz de expresión y las identidades de los clusters
#cluster.markers <- GetAssayData(data_select_bp, slot = "data")
cluster.ids <- Idents(data_select_bp)

# Comparar con la base de datos de referencia
annotations_ms <- SingleR(test = cluster.markers, 
                       ref = ref, 
                       labels = ref$label.fine,
                       clusters = cluster.ids)

# Ver los resultados
head(annotations_ms)
DataFrame with 6 rows and 4 columns
                             scores           labels delta.next
                           <matrix>      <character>  <numeric>
0  0.31891762:0.268169:0.356875:... Oligodendrocytes  0.3285649
1  0.01005668:0.162711:0.233896:...          Neurons  0.0367763
2  0.43070231:0.331161:0.167173:...        Microglia  0.0581364
3 -0.00747119:0.178844:0.244983:...          Neurons  0.0369670
4  0.03368388:0.211730:0.267878:...          Neurons  0.0552592
5  0.02259887:0.194925:0.245688:...          Neurons  0.0433363
     pruned.labels
       <character>
0 Oligodendrocytes
1          Neurons
2        Microglia
3          Neurons
4          Neurons
5          Neurons
Code 
new.cluster.ids <- annotations_ms$labels
names(new.cluster.ids) <- levels(data_select_bp)
data_select_bp_ms <- RenameIdents(data_select_bp, new.cluster.ids)
# Convertir las anotaciones a un data.frame para unirlas con cluster_markers_by_condition
annotations_ms_df <- as.data.frame(annotations_ms)
annotations_ms_df$cluster <- rownames(annotations_ms_df)  # Añadir los IDs de los clusters como columna
# Añadir columna 'labels'
cell_types_ms <- setNames(annotations_ms_df$labels, rownames(annotations_ms_df))
cluster_markers_by_condition$labels_ms <- cell_types_ms[as.character(cluster_markers_by_condition$cluster)]
Code 
# Visualiza con las nuevas identidades
plot <- DimPlot(data_select_bp_ms, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, repel = TRUE)
print(plot)
Code 
save_plot("UMAP_simple_annotation_cluster_plot", plot, width = 12, height = 6)
Code 
#
save_checkpoint(data_select_bp, "11_selected_integrated_annotated_bp")
[CHECKPOINT] Saved: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_11_selected_integrated_annotated_bp.rds  (19:31:41)
Code 
# Save using the name
save(cluster_markers_by_condition, file = paste0("./",output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_markers_by_condition", ".RData"))

#Generate a list 
cluster_list <- cluster_markers_by_condition |> 
  group_split(cluster) |> 
  set_names(paste0("cluster_", unique(cluster_markers_by_condition$cluster)))
#Save xlsx
cluster_list %>% 
write_xlsx(paste0("./",output_path,"/tables/","cluster_list.xlsx"))
#Save RData

# Save using the name
save(cluster_list, file = paste0("./",output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_list", ".RData"))
Code 
rm(reference_median, reference_trimmed, annotations_median, annotations_trimmed, annotations_ms, new.cluster.median, new.cluster.trimmed, new.cluster.ids, data_select_bp_median, data_select_bp_trimmed, data_select_bp_ms, annotations_median_df, annotations_trimmed_df, annotations_ms_df, median_data, median_matrix, trimmed_means_data, trimmed_means_matrix, data_select, ref, cluster.markers, cluster.ids, cell_types_trimmed, cell_types_median, cell_types_ms, plot)
Warning in rm(reference_median, reference_trimmed, annotations_median,
annotations_trimmed, : objeto 'data_select' no encontrado
Code 
gc()
            used (Mb) gc trigger  (Mb)   max used  (Mb)
Ncells  16821125  898   28869927  1542   28869927  1542
Vcells 265136530 2023 1502078194 11460 1877597742 14325

12 Manual Clustering Association

12.1 Brain cell types

We used Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) to visualize all cell clusters and classified and annotated distinct cell types based on known marker genes for each major brain cell type. Following the initial cell clustering, we identified 7 major populations based on the expression of common cell-type markers (Haney et al., 2024): excitatory neurons (Slc17a7), inhibitory neurons (Gad1), oligodendrocytes (Mbp), oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs, Cacng4), microglia (Siglech), astrocytes (Aqp4) and vascular cells (Cldn5) (Figure 67). We then subdivided both excitatory and inhibitory neurons into distinct cell subtypes, annotating them further based on known markers and cortical cytoarchitecture, resulting in 14 clusters (Figure 68). For excitatory neurons, clustering was performed using their gene expression patterns and known markers (Allen Institute, n.d.; Matho et al., 2021), as classification by cortical layer location proved challenging and produced a large number of distinct subtypes. We identified 5 different clusters (Figure 69): ExN1 (Lamp5+ Cux2+) enriched in L2/3, ExN2 (Ndst4+) enriched in L2/3, ExN3 (Rorb+ Cux1+) enriched in L4, ExN4 (Tox+ Hs3st2+) enriched in L4/5, and ExN5 (Tle4+ Fexf2+) enriched in L5/6. We classified inhibitory neurons into subtypes based on previous studies (Bugeon et al., 2022; Tasic et al., 2018; Yao et al., 2021), resulting in four groups: InN1 (Pvalb+), InN2 (Sst+), InN3 (Vip+), and InN4 (Lamp5+).

12.2 Brain cell types

  • Excitatory Neurons: Slc17a7
    • ExN1 enriched L2/3: Lamp5, Cux2
    • ExN2 enriched L2/3: Ndst4
    • ExN3 enriched L4: Rorb, Cux2
    • ExN4 enriched L4/5: Tox, Hs3st2
    • ExN5 enriched L5/6: Tle4, Fexf2
  • Inhibitory Neurons: Gad1
    • InN1: Pvalb
    • InN2: Sst
    • InN3: Vip
    • InN4: Lamp5
  • Oligodendrocytes: Mbp
  • OPC (oligodendrocyte precursor cells): Cacng4
  • Microglia: Siglech
  • Astrocytes: Aqp4
  • Vascular cells: Cldn5

Neuronales:

  • Neuronas de Purkinje: Calbindina (CALB1)
  • Neuronas granulares: GABRA6 (subunidad α6 del receptor GABA-A)
  • Neuronas inhibitorias(interneuronas): Parvalbúmina (PV), Somatostatina (SST), Calretinina(CALB2)
  • Neuronas excitatorias (proyección): Vesicular Glutamate Transporter 1 (VGLUT1), Vesicular Glutamate Transporter 2 (VGLUT2)
  • Neuronas dopaminérgicas: Tirosina hidroxilasa (TH)
  • Neuronas serotoninérgicas: Tryptophan Hydroxylase 2 (TPH2)
  • Neuronas colinérgicas: Colina acetiltransferasa (ChAT)

No neuronales:

  • Astrocitos: Proteína ácida fibrilar glial (GFAP), S100β
  • Oligodendrocitos: Proteína proteolipídica (PLP1), Mielina básica proteína (MBP)
  • Células microgliales: Ionized Calcium Binding Adapter Molecule 1 (IBA1, Aif1), CD11b (Cdk11b),Cx3cr1
  • Células endoteliales: Cluster of Differentiation 31 (CD31), Claudina-5 (CLDN5)

12.2.1 Violin Plot & Features plot

Code 
# Load data_select_bp
data_select_bp <- load_checkpoint("11_selected_integrated_annotated_bp")
[CHECKPOINT] Loaded: output/GSE262881/LogNorm/RData/checkpoint_11_selected_integrated_annotated_bp.rds  (19:31:44)
Code 
Idents(data_select_bp) <- "seurat_clusters"
# Load results ####
load(paste0(output_path,"/RData/cluster_markers_by_condition.RData"))
#Load Cluster_list
load(paste0(output_path,"/RData/cluster_list.RData"))
# Load cluster_markers_by_cluster
load(paste0(output_path, "/RData/", "cluster_markers_by_cluster", ".RData"))
Code 
top1 <- cluster_markers_by_cluster %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  dplyr::filter(avg_log2FC > FC) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  ungroup()
# Cerebellum cell types
features_cerebellum = c("Ppp1r17", "Gabra6", "Eomes", "Lypd6", "Prkcd", "Klhl1", "Lgi2", "Gdf10", "Aqp4", "Mobp", "Ppfibp1", "Dcn", "Kcnj8", "Ttr", "Mrc1", "C1qa", "Flt1", "Foxj1")
# Brain cell types ChatGPT
features_brain = c("Calb1", "Gabra6", "Pvalb", "Sst", "Calb2", "Tph2",  "Gfap", "Plp1","Mbp","Aif1", "Cdk11b", "Cx3cr1", "Pecam1", "Cldn5")

# Brain Cell types FRP
features_brain_frp = c("Slc17a7", "Lamp5", "Cux2", "Ndst4", "Rorb", "Tox", "Hs3st2", "Tle4", "Fexf2", "Gad1", "Pvalb", "Sst", "Vip", "Cacng4", "Siglech", "Cx3cr1", "Aqp4", "Cldn5")

features <- c(features_brain, features_brain_frp)
features <- unique(features)


features_presents <- features[features %in% rownames(data_select_bp)]

for(feature in features_presents){
  #VlnPlot
  plot<- VlnPlot(data_select_bp, features = feature, split.by = "condition")
  print(plot)
  save_plot(paste0("Vln_plot_",feature), plot)
  #UMAP by features
  feature_plot <- FeaturePlot(data_select_bp, features = feature, reduction = "umap")
  print(feature_plot)
  save_plot("Feature_plot_Markers", feature_plot,width = 8, height = 6)
}
The default behaviour of split.by has changed.
Separate violin plots are now plotted side-by-side.
To restore the old behaviour of a single split violin,
set split.plot = TRUE.
      
This message will be shown once per session.

12.3 Stacked Violin Plot

Code 
plot_stacked_violin <- function(seurat_obj, features, split_by = "condition",
                                assay = "RNA", title = NULL) {
  features <- intersect(features, rownames(seurat_obj))
  vln <- VlnPlot(seurat_obj, features = features,
                 stack = TRUE, flip = TRUE, pt.size = 0.2, raster = FALSE,
                 fill.by = "ident", split.by = split_by, split.plot = FALSE,
                 assay = assay)
  if (!is.null(title)) vln <- vln + ggtitle(title)
  vln
}

vln_stacked <- plot_stacked_violin(data_select_bp, features = features)
print(vln_stacked)
Code 
save_plot("Stacked_VlnPlot_markers", vln_stacked, width = 8, height = 12)

12.4 DotPlot

Code 
dotplot <- DotPlot(data_select_bp, features = features_presents, cols = c("blue", "red"), dot.scale = 6, split.by = "condition") +
    RotatedAxis()
print(dotplot)
Code 
save_plot("DotPlot_features", dotplot, width = 16 ,height = 18)

12.5 Clustering

Cluster Main marker Cell type Subtype
0 Slc17a7
1 Plp1, Mbp, Oligodendrocytes
2 Calb1, Slc17a7, Lamp5, Cux2, Rorb Excitatory
3 Cx3cr1, Siglech Microglia
4 Gad1, Sst, Cux2 Inhibitory
5 Calb1, Tle4, Gad1, Cacng4 Inhibitory
6 Slc17a7, Tox4, Hs3st2, Tle4 Excitatory
7 Gad1 Inhibitory
8 Slc17a7, Tox4, Hs3st2 Excitatory
9 Rorb, Tox, Tle4, Aqp4 Astrocytes
10 Calb1, Slc17a7, Cux2 Excitatory
11 Tox, Cacng4 Pericytes

Code 
  # Apply manual labels
  data_select_bp <- RenameIdents(data_select_bp, new_cluster_ids)

  # Store the manual label back into metadata (useful for downstream DE)
  data_select_bp$cell_type_manual <- Idents(data_select_bp)

  # Visualise
   plot_manual <- DimPlot(data_select_bp, reduction = "umap",
                         label = TRUE, repel = TRUE, pt.size = 0.5)
  print(plot_manual)
Code 
  save_plot("UMAP_manual_annotation", plot_manual, width = 12, height = 6)

rm(list = setdiff(ls(), keep_objects))

19 HeatMaps

Code 
source("../code/heatmap.R")

  # DE genes, all clusters
  heatmap_de(data_select_bp, cluster_markers_by_condition)
Code 
  # DE genes, only clusters 0, 2 and 5, magma palette, top 30 genes
  heatmap_de(data_select_bp, cluster_markers_by_condition,
             clusters = c(0, 2, 5), n_top = 30, palette = "magma")
Code 
  # Custom genes — single combined heatmap across clusters 0 and 3
  heatmap_genes(data_select_bp, c("Gfap","Cx3cr1","Mbp","Pvalb"),
                clusters = c(0, 3))
Code 
  # Custom genes — one heatmap per cluster (named clusters also work)
 # heatmap_genes(data_select_bp, c("Gfap","Cx3cr1","Mbp"), clusters = c("Astrocytes","Microglia"), per_cluster = TRUE)
Code 
source("../code/heatmap.R")
  # All clusters, all conditions — mean expression of a gene set
  heatmap_mean(data_select_bp, c("Gfap","Cx3cr1","Mbp","Pvalb","Aqp4"))
Code 
  my_genes <- c("Gfap","Cx3cr1","Mbp","Pvalb","Aqp4", "Gad1", "Slc17a7", "Lamp5")
  # Only clusters 0, 2 and 5
  heatmap_mean(data_select_bp, my_genes, clusters = c(0, 2, 5))
Code 
  # Raw mean (no z-score) — shows actual log-normalised values
  heatmap_mean(data_select_bp, my_genes, scale_rows = TRUE, palette = "viridis")
